content.problemStatement:
Cherry has a binary matrix AAA consisting of NNN rows and MMM columns. The rows are numbered from 111 to NNN, columns are numbered from 111 to MMM. Element at row iii (111 ≤ iii ≤ NNN) and column jjj (111 ≤ jjj ≤ MMM) is denoted as AijA_{ij}Aij​. All elements of AAA are either 000 or 111.

He performs QQQ queries on matrix. Each query is provided by four integers x1x_{1}x1​, y1y_{1}y1​, x2x_{2}x2​, y2y_{2}y2​ which define the rectangle, where (x1x_{1}x1​, y1y_{1}y1​) stands for the coordinates of the top left cell of the rectangle, while (x2x_{2}x2​, y2y_{2}y2​) stands for the coordinates of the bottom right cell. You need to flip all the bits i.e. (000 to 111, 111 to 000) that are located fully inside the query rectangle.

Finally, print the matrix after performing all the queries.

Note: x1x_{1}x1​ represents the row number while y1y_{1}y1​ represents the column number.

###Input:


The first line of the input contains two integers NNN and MMM — the number of rows and the number of columns in the matrix.
Each of the next NNN lines contains a string of length MMM, where the jthj^{th}jth character of ithi^{th}ith line denotes the value of Ai,jA_{i,j}Ai,j​.
Next line contains an integer QQQ — the number of queries.
Then follow QQQ lines with queries descriptions. Each of them contains four space-seperated integers x1x_{1}x1​, y1y_{1}y1​, x2x_{2}x2​, y2y_{2}y2​ — coordinates of the up left and bottom right cells of the query rectangle.
###Output:

Print the matrix, in the form of NNN strings, after performing all the queries.

###Constraints


1≤N,M≤10001 \leq N,M \leq 10001≤N,M≤1000
0≤Aij≤10 \leq  A_{ij}  \leq 10≤Aij​≤1
1≤Q≤1061 \leq Q \leq 10^61≤Q≤106
1≤x1≤x2≤N1 \leq x_{1} \leq x_{2} \leq N1≤x1​≤x2​≤N
1≤y1≤y2≤M1 \leq y_{1} \leq y_{2} \leq M1≤y1​≤y2​≤M
constraints: 0 ≤ Q ≤ 10^6 (sum of Q over all test cases fits in memory)
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T — the number of test cases. For each test case: first line contains two integers N and M (1 ≤ N, M ≤ 1000) — number of rows and columns. The next N lines each contain a string of length M consisting of characters '0' or '1', describing the initial matrix. The following line contains an integer Q (0 ≤ Q ≤ 10^6) — number of queries. Each of the next Q lines contains four integers x1 y1 x2 y2 (1 ≤ x1 ≤ x2 ≤ N, 1 ≤ y1 ≤ y2 ≤ M) describing a rectangle whose cells must be flipped.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output N lines, each a string of length M, representing the matrix after all queries have been applied.

sampleTestCases.explanation: Initial matrix is all zeros. After first query (1,1)-(1,1) cell (1,1) becomes 1. After second query (2,2)-(2,2) cell (2,2) becomes 1. After third query flips the whole matrix, turning 1->0 and 0->1, resulting in rows "01" and "10".
First query flips rectangle rows 1‑3, columns 2‑3. Second query flips row 2, columns 1‑4. Applying both yields the shown matrix.
Only one row. Flipping columns 2‑4 changes bits 0→1, 1→0, 0→1, resulting in 11010.

solution.approach: Flipping a cell an even number of times leaves it unchanged, an odd number of times toggles it. We can treat each query as adding 1 (mod 2) to every cell inside its rectangle. Using a 2‑D difference array we can apply each rectangle in O(1): diff[x1][y1] ^= 1, diff[x1][y2+1] ^= 1, diff[x2+1][y1] ^= 1, diff[x2+1][y2+1] ^= 1 (using XOR because we only care about parity). After all queries, compute the prefix XOR (or prefix sum modulo 2) over rows then columns to obtain for each cell the total number of flips modulo 2. Finally, if flips[i][j] is 1 we toggle the original bit, otherwise we keep it. This runs in O(N·M + Q) time and O(N·M) extra space.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N·M) auxiliary space per test case
